The K.C.I.E. Entry: British Imperial Honours in the Pañjī 
The entry for Mahārājādhirāja Kāmeśvara Siṃha (page 566) is the only 
instance in all three volumes where a British imperial honour is recorded 
within the genealogical matrix. 'Vivdhityādi Mahārāja Bahādur Kāmeśvara 
Siṃha (K.C.I.E. Litt.)' — the parenthetical notation K.C.I.E. (Knight 
Commander of the Indian Empire) alongside 'Litt.' (Doctor of Letters, 
honorary) places this ruler at the intersection of Mithilā kingship and British 
colonial recognition. The Pañjī records this not as a biographical footnote but 
within the standard suta-chain notation, treating imperial honours as 
equivalent to the traditional Vivdhityādi and Mahārājādhirāja designations. 
This is the genealogical system absorbing colonial modernity without 
rupture. 
The Sweden Inter-Faith Marriage 
Page 286, within the Bābu Khaḍagapati Siṃha chain, preserves in 
parenthesis: '(Bābu Khaḍagapati Siṃha sutā Svīḍana deśe antadhārmika 
vivāha:)' — 'The daughters/sons of Bābu Khaḍagapati Siṃha [performed] an 
inter-faith marriage in the country of Sweden.' The term 'antadhārmika' 
(between-religions) is a euphemism for inter-religious marriage. The Pañjī,

which exists to certify caste endogamy, here records its own transgression 
without comment — the parenthetical marker rather than the standard vivāha 
formula suggests the Panjikar acknowledged the marriage without fully 
integrating it into the genealogical chain. The son Bābu Jīveśvara Siṃha's 
subsequent entry follows normally, confirming the family's continued 
participation in the Pañjī system despite the inter-faith union. 
The Anterjātiya Entry 
A second extraordinary inter-caste entry appears at page 573: '(antajātiya 
vivāha:)' — 'inter-caste marriage' — recorded parenthetically for Śrī 
Dīkanandana Siṃha's sons. Like the Sweden entry, this is preserved in 
brackets, marked as exceptional but not expunged. These two entries together 
document the Pañjī tradition's pragmatic response to social change: 
acknowledge the deviance, bracket it formally, continue the lineage. 
3.2 The Abhinavā-Sarasvatī Title Complex 
Page 301, within the Fnandaha-Sodarapura cluster, preserves a remarkable 
scholarly title chain: 'Sarvadātā Mahāmahopādhyāya Raghunātha suta 
Mahāmahopādhyāya Bhavānīdatta … Mahopādhyāya Pītāmbara sutā 
Abhinava-Sarasvatī-dāṅkit Mahāmahopādhyāya Hilocana, Mahopādhyāya 
Daṇḍapāṇi alias Ghañjaya, Mahopādhyāya Ṭīkākāraka Gaukuḷanātha, 
Sudopādhyāya Jagannātha.' This passage, covering a single family's scholarly 
output, contains no fewer than seven distinct Mahopādhyāya-level titles in 
one lineage, including: 
Title Compound | Meaning | Novelty 
sarvasvadātā / Sarvadātā | 'The giver of everything' — ultimate patronage 
epithet | Highest patronage designation; Khaṇḍa 1 had Sarvajña Garība ('all-
knowing pauper'), here the opposite extreme 
abhinava sarasvatī dāṅkita / Abhinava-Sarasvatī-dāṅkit | 'Bearing the seal of 
the New/Fresh Sarasvatī' | Variant of Avalaṃba-Sarasvatī from pages 66–
347; this is 'Abhinava' (new/fresh) not 'Avalaṃba' (supportive) — a distinct 
honor 
ṭīkakāraka / Ṭīkākāraka | 'Commentary-maker' — scholarly commentator | 
Professional designation for a scholar whose primary work is writing 
commentaries (ṭīkā) 
sudopādhyāya / Sudopādhyāya | 'Good/excellent Upādhyāya' — merit 
designation | Rare honorific prefix emphasizing quality of scholarship rather 
than quantity

3.3 The Calcutta University Maithili Entry 
Page 290, within the Mahiṣī cluster, contains an entry of direct relevance to 
Maithili literary history: 'Mahopādhyāya Rāja alias Śivadatta, Jyotiprvid 
Achhu [Upādhyāya Tī (7/05)] Vaiyākaraṇa Prādhyāpaka (Calcutta University 
Maithily Deptt.) Khadabhī' — 'Rāja alias Śivadatta, Jyotirvid-scholar Achhu, 
[alongside] the Vaiyākaraṇa (Grammarian) who served as Professor 
(Prādhyāpaka) in the Maithili Department of Calcutta University.' This is the 
first known Pañjī record of an individual holding an academic post 
specifically in a Maithili language department — linking the genealogical 
tradition to the emergence of Maithili as a university subject in the colonial 
period. The entry occurs within a family whose compound titles span 
Jyotirvid (astrological scholar), Vaiyākaraṇa (grammarian), and Prādhyāpaka 
(university professor), suggesting a family tradition of evolving from 
traditional to modern academic scholarship. 
3.4 Mahiṣī as Scholarly Satellite 
The village Mahiṣī (mahiṣī, modern Mahishi, Saharsa district) emerges in 
pages 348–690 as a distinctive scholarly satellite in the Pañjī's eastern 
geographic range. Mahiṣī is historically known as the seat of the scholar 
Maṇḍana Miśra (the opponent of Ādi Śaṃkara in the classical Mīmāṃsā-
Advaita debate) and as a centre of Mīmāṃsā scholarship. The Pañjī entries 
connecting Mahiṣī to Budhavāla and Darihara clusters confirm that the 
village maintained its scholarly reputation into the 19th and 20th centuries — 
the families from Mahiṣī in this section carry Vaiyākaraṇa (grammarian) 
titles and appear in the Calcutta University Maithili Department entry above. 
3.5 The Rāya Families: Mughal-to-British Administrative 
Continuity 
The Rāya (rāya / rāya) title appears 53 times in pages 348–690, concentrated 
particularly in the Sūragana–Jālaya–Darihara corridor. Unlike the Ṭhākura

records.text:

Gajendra Thakur — 243 
prefix (which marks high-status Brahmin ritual families), the Rāya prefix 
marks families in revenue administration — a lineage of service stretching 
from the Mughal amīl system through colonial zamīndārī. Notable Rāya 
families in this section: 
• 
Rāya Rūdranārāyaṇa (p.) — connected to Mahārāja Samara Siṃha via 
Suragana; sons Rāya Rāmadeva, Rāya Jayadeva, Rāya Bhavadeva 
represent three brothers in simultaneous administrative service. 
• 
Rāya Candra Nārāyaṇa (p.) — recorded with sons 'Rāja Devanārāyaṇa, 
Rāja Śrīnārāyaṇa, Nārāyaṇa, Rāya Lalīta Nārāyaṇa, Rāya 
Kīrtimunārāyaṇa' — five sons all bearing royal-administrative names, 
suggesting a family policy of naming sons for continued administrative 
legitimacy. 
• 
Rāya Vāgīśa (p.) — connected to Mahopādhyāya lineage via Valiyāsa; 
transitions from revenue-officer to scholarly family within one 
generation. 
3.6 The Kavi Jīvakarṇa Entry: A Poet in the Pañjī 
Page 407 contains a unique designation: 'Bhaveśa suto jīvakarṇu: baulyasaṃ 
balabhara suta maheśa dau' — identifying Jīvakarṇa as a 'kavi' (poet) in 
subsequent entries. A few lines later, his genealogical chain produces 
'Jīvakarṇa suto Rūradatta, Bhairapadatta' — the family continues in the 
register. The designation of a named poet within the Pañjī's suta-chain, rather 
than merely noting the family, echoes the earlier Kavi Vidyāpati placement. 
These poet-entries in the Pañjī suggest the Panjikar treated literary excellence 
as a genealogical credential alongside scholarly titles and administrative 
positions. 
3.7 Mādhurakavi Māghava: The 'Sweet Poet' Designation 
Page 407 also preserves an extraordinary compound: 'Amaru suto mahāī 
mahāī sutā Mādhurakavi Māghava Yaddhū Maṇināthā' — listing 
'Mādhurakavi Māghava' (the sweet/melodious poet Māghava) as a personal 
designation within a genealogical chain. The compound Mādhurakavi — 
'sweet poet' or 'poet of sweetness' — is a title indicating mastery of the 
śṛṅgāra (erotic/romantic) and madhura (sweet) aesthetic registers. Its 
appearance in the Pañjī alongside brothers Yaddhū and Maṇinātha confirms 
that poetic excellence was treated as a heritable family credential in Mithilā's 
social economy.

The presence of six-marriage colophons (ṣaṣṭhama vivāha) for two different 
individuals in this volume is without precedent in the previous two volumes. 
These reflect the historical reality of serial marriage among upper-caste men 
— whether due to wife's death, childlessness, or social ambition — and the 
Pañjī's duty-bound recording of each marriage as a genealogical fact. The 
parenthetical treatment of the Sweden inter-faith and inter-caste marriages 
demonstrates that the Pañjī had no formal mechanism for exclusion, only for 
quarantine via the bracket notation.

7. Gallery of Unique Entries 
7.1 'Raja Ramalochana (Katihar)' — Urban Identity Marker 
Page 389 records 'Mahipati suto Rāja Rāmacandra (Kaṭihāra) Mahāū 
Khauāla saṃ Bhaiyā dau' — placing 'Rāja Rāmacandra' with the identifier 
'Kaṭihāra' (Katihar), a major junction city and district headquarters in eastern 
Bihar. This is not a village but an urban administrative centre — its use as a 
genealogical identifier (vāsī-equivalent) reflects the family's urban relocation 
and the Pañjī's pragmatic adaptation: when a family moves to a city rather 
than a village, the city name is recorded in place of the mūla-grāma.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 248 
7.2 'Kolkata (Pāli)' — Calcutta as a Mūla Reference 
Page 351 contains 'Koliṭṭā Pāli saṃ Maheśa dau' — using 'Kolkata (Pāli)' as a 
dau-source, where 'Pāli' is the sub-locality within Kolkata where the family 
maintained their Mithilā-community identity. This is the Pañjī's way of 
recording that a family's genealogical authority for a marriage-connection 
passed through their Calcutta community branch. The use of 'Pāli' alongside 
'Koliṭṭā' suggests a specific Maithili neighbourhood or community cluster 
within the city — a Mithilā diaspora enclave in colonial Calcutta. 
7.3 'Sannha-vāsī Karmaharā' — Multi-Compound Residential 
Designation 
Page 351 also contains 'Sannavāsī Karmaharā saṃ Mukunda suta Harapati 
dau' — 'from Sannha-resident [family] at Karmaharā via Mukunda's son 
Harapati.' The 'Sannavāsī' designation (resident of Sannha — possibly a 
neighbourhood or hamlet) combined with Karmaharā as the mūla creates a 
hyper-specific three-level geographic identifier: district → village → sub-
village residential cluster. This granularity is unusual even within the Pañjī's 
detailed geographic tracking. 
7.4 The Llavakāra-dāṅkit Entry Revisited 
Page 676, near the document's end, contains a compressed entry: 'Rāya 
Rāmadeva sutā … ālī saṃ Llavakāra dāṃ. Bhavānātha' — the 'Llavakāra-
dāṅkit' (administrative-officer seal) title appearing again, this time for a 
Rāya-family member. This confirms that the Llavakāra designation, first seen 
in pages 66–347, remained in active use through the later portions of the 
corpus and applied to revenue-officer as well as scholarly families. 
7.5 Vaṃśī, Ganesha, Hariyama — The Triadic Dau Formula 
Throughout pages 348–690, a recurring triadic dau formula appears: 'Vaṃśī 
saṃ X dau — Ganeśa saṃ Y dau — Hariyama saṃ Z dau' — where three 
villages serve as sequential dau-sources for siblings within the same family. 
This triadic pattern, where one family's three sons take wives from three 
distinct sub-clusters, is more common in this volume than in the previous 
two. It reflects the increased geographic spread of marriage networks in the 
later genealogical layers, as families became dispersed enough that no single 
mūla-cluster could supply all marriage partners.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 249 
8. Analytical Synthesis 
8.1 The Pañjī Meets Indian Modernity 
Pages 348–690 document the Panji Prabandha tradition's encounter with 
Indian modernity in its fullest form. Within a single volume, the same 
genealogical notation that was designed to track Brahmin family-status in 
12th-century Mithilā is used to record a British imperial knighthood 
(K.C.I.E.), a Deputy Inspector General of Police, an Income Tax 
Commissioner, a Calcutta University professor, and an inter-faith marriage in 
Sweden. The system does not break — it bends. Imperial honours are treated 
like Vivdhityādi epithets; university posts are treated like Mahopādhyāya 
titles; urban residence (Kolkata, Katihar) is treated like a mūla-grāma. The 
Pañjī's capacity for absorption is its most remarkable institutional feature. 
8.2 Sodarapura as the New Gravitational Centre 
The emergence of Sodarapura (853 appearances) as the dominant village in 
this volume has a specific historical explanation. Sodarapura (modern 
Sudarpur) lies at the confluence of several Brahmin settlement zones in the 
Sitamarhi–Madhubani corridor. By the 18th–19th century — the period when 
most of the genealogical chains in this volume were crystallising — 
Sodarapura had become a primary registration point for families seeking 
Panjikar certification. Its dominance reflects the administrative geography of 
the Pañjī tradition itself: when families from diverse sub-clusters needed to 
formalise a marriage connection, Sodarapura's Pankikar office was the 
primary referral node. 
8.3 Royal Legitimation at Scale 
The 91 Mahārāja-title entries in pages 348–690 — compared to 41 in pages 
66–347 — reflect the expansion of the royal genealogical complex from a 
single Mādhavasiṃha entry to a multi-branched dynastic record spanning 
Chhatrasiṃha, 
Rāmeśvarasiṃha, 
Kāmeśvarasiṃha, 
Maheśvarasiṃha, 
Lakṣmeśvarasiṃha, and numerous collateral branches. The Pañjī here 
functions as a royal chronicle within a genealogical register — each new 
branch needs Panjikar certification of its membership in the dau-bīji network, 
and each certification is recorded. By the time Kāmeśvarasiṃha receives his 
K.C.I.E., the royal family has been in the Pañjī for at least six generations.

8.4 The Complete Vivāha Sub-Registry 
The 41 marriage colophons in pages 348–690 — including multi-marriage 
records for individuals with 4, 5, and 6 documented marriages — constitute a 
sub-registry of Mithilā elite matrimonial history spanning roughly 1880 to 
2004 CE. The Saṃvat 1337 date on Girdhārī Siṃha's second marriage (= 
1880 CE) provides the earliest dateable colophon in this volume, while the 
September 2004 CE date on Hemadatta's second marriage provides the latest. 
This 124-year range of matrimonial records, embedded within genealogical 
chains that trace back to the 12th century, makes pages 348–690 the single 
most temporally rich section of the entire Panji Prabandha.

10. Concluding Note 
Pages 348–690 of the Panji Prabandha complete what may be called the 
Pañjī's 'long 19th century' — the period in which the 12th-century 
genealogical system, designed for a stable agrarian Brahmin community in 
the Tirhut heartland, was forced to accommodate the full pressure of Indian 
modernity: British imperial administration, urban migration to Calcutta, 
university education, inter-faith marriage abroad, and the emergence of 
professional careers (police officer, income tax commissioner, university 
professor) that had no traditional equivalent. The Pañjī absorbed all of this 
without structural collapse — not because it was infinitely flexible, but 
because its core technology (the suta/dau notation within a mūla-grāma 
matrix) was robust enough to treat any social fact as a genealogical 
coordinate. 
The 853 appearances of Sodarapura, the 91 royal-title entries, the 41 
marriage colophons, and the three extraordinary entries (Sweden inter-faith 
marriage, Calcutta University Maithili professorship, K.C.I.E. knighthood) 
make pages 348–690 the single most historically information-dense section 
of the Panji Prabandha. Together with the two previous volumes, they 
constitute a genealogical record of extraordinary depth — eight centuries of 
Maithil social history encoded in a compact, precise notation system that 
proved adaptable enough to survive from the Karnāṭa kingdom to the 
Swedish diaspora.

Chapter 12 
Mithila's Living Genome: Pañjī Prabandha Volume I — 
Section 2 (Uṭīp Sub-Commentary) 
MITHILA'S LIVING GENOME 
A Comprehensive Scholarly Report on 
Genome Mapping (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.): The Panjī Prabandha of 
Mithila 
[jīnoma maipiṃga (4 e.ḍī. sa~ 2 e.ḍī.) – mithilāka pañjī prabandha] 
Volume II, Section 2 (Uṭīp / Sub-Commentary) 
Prepared as an Analytical and Explanatory Scholarly Report 
Drawing upon Anthropological, Sociological, Genealogical, 
and Historical Frameworks 
Source Document Series: 
Videha Parallel Literature Movement 
Gajendra Thakur, Founder-Editor 
1. THE DOCUMENT: IDENTITY AND ARCHITECTURE 
1.1 What Is This Document? 
The text under examination is Volume II, Section 2 (Khaṇḍa 2) of a 
monumental work titled Jīnom Maipiṃg (450 A.D. to 2009 A.D.) — 
Mithilāk Pañjī Prabandha, translatable as "Genome Mapping (450 A.D. to 
2009 A.D.) — The Genealogical Record Management of Mithila." The 
section presented is the Uṭīp, which in Maithili scholarly tradition means 
"sub-commentary" or "supplementary lineage record."

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 253 
The framing of genealogical data as "genome mapping" is a deliberate 
intellectual move by the editor: it asserts that traditional Pañjī is, in essence, 
an indigenous biotechnology — a continuous, living database of human 
genetic and social inheritance, maintained by a dedicated priestly-scholarly 
class across fifteen-plus centuries. 
Full Title | Jīnom Maipiṃg (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.) – Mithilāk Pañjī 
Prabandha 
Volume | II 
Section | Khaṇḍa 2 (khaṇḍa 2) — Uṭīp (upaṭīpa) 
Script | Devanagari (Maithili dialect conventions) 
Language | Classical Maithili (genealogical register) 
Page Range | 2.2 to 2.72 (71 pages of dense genealogical text) 
Sub-sections | 155+ numbered Uṭīp sub-entries across villages and lineages 
Families / Lineage Trees | Several hundred individual family trees (vaṃśa-
vṛkṣa) 
Temporal Span | Approximately 450 A.D. to 2009 A.D. (1,559 years) 
Geographical Coverage | Mithila region (present-day North Bihar, India; and 
Terai Nepal) 
Publication Context | Videha Parallel Literature Movement 
1.2 The Genre: Pañjī Prabandha 
The Pañjī Prabandha (pañjī prabandha) is one of the most remarkable 
indigenous record-keeping systems in world history. It is a hereditary 
genealogical register maintained by a caste of specialists known as Pañjīkāra 
or Pañjīkār, who function as official custodians of patrilineal and 
matrimonial data among Maithili Brahmin communities. 
Unlike European parish records or Chinese clan genealogies, the Pañjī is a 
living, transactional document: it is consulted and updated at every marriage 
negotiation to verify gotra (patrilineal exogamous clan), mūl (root lineage), 
pravara (Vedic sage-ancestry), and matrimonial compatibility across an 
enormous kinship network. The Pañjīkār acts as a human search engine — 
or, in the document's own framing, a genetic database manager. 
Key Concept:The Pañjī system is not archival in the Western sense; it is 
operational genealogy. Its purpose is not merely commemoration but active 
social governance — determining who may marry whom across hundreds of 
villages and dozens of generations.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 254 
1.3 The Uṭīp (upaṭīpa) Sub-genre 
The specific section before us is titled Uṭīp, meaning "sub-note" or 
"supplementary annotation." Within the Pañjī architecture: 
• 
The main Pañjī (Mūl Pañjī) records primary lineages of high-status 
Maithili Brahmin families. 
• 
The Uṭīp records secondary, collateral, and migrant branches — families 
who have moved to new villages, lineages that branched from the main 
stem, or sub-families whose records needed a supplementary volume. 
• 
The Uṭīp is therefore a genealogical "overflow" or "index extension" — 
essential for completeness, and in practice indispensable because 
Mithila's Brahmin population spread across hundreds of villages through 
strategic marriage migration. 
The section is further divided by geographic circuit: Sakkari-Sārisab-Pāhīṭol, 
Digauṃdh, Kanhauli, Mānī, Hāṭī, Tetraharā, Mahiyā, Mahiṣī, Maṃgaraunī, 
Barlirājpur, Naḍuār, Bheṭ, Rurauculī, Tarauni, Jhañjhārpur, Analapur, 
Gaṃgaurā, Ḍumrā, Simarivāṛ, Murajpur, Raṭaulī, Ṭarauni, and many others 
— each a village or village-cluster in the Mithila heartland. 
2. MITHILA AND THE MAITHILI BRAHMIN WORLD 
2.1 Geographical and Historical Setting 
Mithila (mithilā) is a cultural-linguistic region straddling the North Bihar 
plains of India and the eastern Terai of Nepal. Bounded roughly by the 
Gandak (Narayani) river to the west, the Mahananda/Kosi to the east, the 
Himalayan foothills to the north, and the Gangetic plain to the south, it 
encompasses a zone of extraordinary cultural continuity. The mythological 
capital is Janakpur (in Nepal), home of King Janaka, father of Sītā. 
This region was the seat of one of the most intellectually sophisticated 
Brahmin communities in South Asia. Mithila produced Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya 
(author of the Tattvacintāmaṇi, the foundational text of Navya-Nyāya logic), 
Vidyāpati (the great Maithili poet), and Maṇḍana Miśra. The Maithili 
Brahmin community — especially the Śrotriyas and Yogyas — developed 
institutional mechanisms of extraordinary sophistication to maintain their 
social identity across millennia.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 255 
2.2 Caste Stratification Within Maithili Brahmins 
The Pañjī system is specifically associated with Maithili Brahmins, who are 
themselves internally stratified. Understanding this stratification is crucial to 
reading the document: 
Śrotriya | The highest-rank Maithili Brahmins; fullest Pañjī regulation 
applied; marriage choices most restricted 
Yogya | Second tier; also regulated by Pañjī but with slightly wider 
permissible marriage circles 
Paṃjī-baddha | "Pañjī-bound" — all families whose marriages are recorded 
and regulated by the system 
Pañjīkāra | The hereditary custodian-scribes who maintain and interpret the 
Pañjī 
Mūl | Root lineage — the fundamental genealogical identity unit 
Gotra | Patrilineal exogamous clan, traced to a Vedic sage (ṛṣi) 
Pravara | Series of ancestral sages invoked in ritual; determines marriage 
prohibitions 
Designations appearing throughout the document confirm this stratification: 
persons are identified as Vaidika (vaidika — Vedic scholar), Vaidya (vaidya 
— 
physician/medical 
practitioner), 
Mahāmahopādhyāya 
(mahāmahopādhyāya — the highest traditional scholarly title), and Pañḍita 
(paṃḍita — learned man). These are not decorative epithets but functional 
social identifiers that determine ritual rank and marriage eligibility. 
2.3 The Social Logic of Endogamy and Exogamy 
The Pañjī system navigates a complex dual imperative: 
Exogamy (gotra rule):A man and woman of the same gotra (patrilineal 
clan) may NEVER marry — this is a categorical prohibition treated as 
equivalent to incest. The Pañjī identifies gotra at every step. 
Endogamy (community rule):Marriage must be within the broadly defined 
Maithili Brahmin community, and more specifically within compatible sub-
tiers of the hierarchy. 
The result is a highly constrained combinatorial problem: find a suitable 
spouse across hundreds of villages, satisfying both gotra-exogamy and 
community-endogamy, without repeating a marriage alliance too recently 
(the "sapinḍa" prohibition). This is precisely what the Pañjī solves — it is a

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 256 
medieval information system for solving a massive, recurring constraint-
satisfaction problem across a geographically dispersed community. 
3. THE GENEALOGICAL GRAMMAR: HOW TO READ 
THE ENTRIES 
3.1 The Formal Structure of Each Entry 
Every entry in this Uṭīp follows a rigidly standardized formula, written in a 
dense, abbreviated Classical Maithili. Once the grammar is decoded, each 
line yields a precise genealogical statement. The fundamental unit is: 
Format:[Father's name] suto/suta [Son(s)/Daughter(s)] [Village/Location] 
saṃ [Mother's lineage source — donor's name] dau [Village of maternal 
grandfather] ddau 
Let us parse the standard vocabulary: 
suto (suto) | "Son of" — masculine singular child (one son) 
sutau (sutau) | "Two sons of" — masculine dual (exactly two sons) 
suta (sutā) | "Sons/children of" — masculine plural (three or more sons) 
sutāśca | "Sons and daughters of" — indicating mixed male-female progeny 
dau (dau) | "Dau" — abbreviation of dauhadra or dauhi — the maternal side 
donor 
ddau (ddau) | "Maternal grandfather" — the father of the mother 
saṃ (sa~) | "From" — indicating provenance of the bride (village origin) 
apara (aparā) | "Another / additional" — marking a polygamous second or 
third wife's children 
mūl/pravara indicator | Numbers in parentheses like (286//01) — Pañjī 
page/entry reference numbers 
pra (pra) | Abbreviation for "prāyukta" — also known as / alias 
ātma (ātmārāma etc.) | "Self" names — used when someone's alias or second 
name follows 
3.2 The Parenthetical Number System 
Throughout the text, every married couple entry is followed by parenthetical 
numbers such as (286//0), (202//0), (109/0), etc. These are cross-reference 
codes to the main Pañjī volumes, indicating:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 257 
• 
The volume/page number in the primary Pañjī register where the 
maternal lineage is recorded 
• 
Sub-entry identifiers within that page 
• 
These codes allow the Pañjīkāra to cross-verify any claim by consulting 
the master register 
This numerical cross-referencing system is essentially a pre-digital relational 
database — primary keys linking entries across multiple volumes. It prevents 
fraud, ensures consistency, and allows rapid lookup of any individual's 
complete ancestry across both paternal and maternal lines. 
3.3 Village Identifiers as Lineage Addresses 
Every individual and every married pair is associated with a specific village. 
Villages that appear as sources of brides or maternal lines include: 
• 
Sārisab (sarisaba) — repeatedly cited; a major lineage-hub village in 
Madhubani district 
• 
Darbhangā (dariharā) — major city and cultural center of Mithila 
• 
Khaṇḍabalā (khaṇḍabalā) — another lineage-hub; associated with the 
Siṃha (Singh) Kāyastha nobility 
• 
Māṇḍar (māṇḍara), Pābaulī (pabaulī), Saudāpur (sodarapura) — major 
donor villages 
• 
Narauna (narauna), Pālī (pālī), Budhavāla (budhavāla), Hariyam 
(hariamba) — frequently appearing 
• 
Karmaharā (karamahā), Ekharā (ekaharā), Sakrāṛhī (sakarāḍhī), Ghosauṭ 
(ghosota) 
• 
Siṃhaulī (siṃhaulī), Alaya (alaya), Kujaulī (kujaulī), Kumarī (kumārī) 
In the Pañjī system, a village is not merely a geographical location — it is a 
genealogical coordinate. Knowing that someone comes "from Sārisab, 
Māṇḍar saṃ" immediately narrows the field of possible lineages and helps 
the Pañjīkāra locate the relevant entries. 
3.4 Multi-Wife (Apara) Notations 
The repeated use of "apara" (aparā — "another") throughout the text 
documents polygamy systematically. When a man had multiple wives, each 
wife's children are listed separately under the same father's name, with 
"apara" marking the subsequent wives. This is non-judgmental recording —

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 258 
the Pañjī does not comment on polygamy but simply traces all biological 
descent lines for matrimonial verification purposes. 
This practice has a direct genealogical consequence: multiple parallel descent 
lines from one man, each with different maternal lineages (gotras, villages), 
creating complex kinship webs that only the Pañjī can fully document. 
4. PROMINENT FAMILIES, TITLES, AND NOTABLE 
INDIVIDUALS 
4.1 The Institutional Title System 
The document is studded with honorific titles that function as social 
metadata. Understanding them reveals the professional and ritual 
stratification of Maithili society: 
Mahāmahopādhyāya (mahāmahopādhyāya) | The highest traditional 
scholarly title in Sanskrit learning; equivalent to a distinguished professor or 
Doctor of Letters. Extremely rare. 
Mahopādhyāya (mahopādhyāya) | Senior scholar of Sanskrit/traditional 
learning 
Vaidyakaraṇī (vaiyākaraṇi) | Specialist in Sanskrit grammar (vyākaraṇa); 
grammatician 
Jyautiṣī (jyotiṣī / jyo.) | Astrologer-astronomer; officiates at calendrical and 
ritual functions 
Māmāṃśaka (mīmāṃśaka / rimmāṃśaka) | Specialist in Mīmāṃsā — Vedic 
exegesis/ritualistic philosophy 
Sadu (sadu) | Abbreviation indicating a Sadupādhyāya — a sub-specialist 
scholar grade 
Vaidika (vaidika) | Vedic ritualist; one who performs or maintains Vedic 
sacrificial traditions 
Kavī (karavi) | Poet; indicates literary accomplishment 
Ṭhākur (ṭhākura) | Title for Kāyastha (scribal/administrative) nobility, not 
Brahmin 
Rāja / Kumāra (rājā / kumāra) | Royal/noble title — appears for Rāja 
families like Vijay Govinda Singh 
D.G.P. / M.B.B.S. / M.A. | Modern professional credentials — showing the 
document spans into the modern era

Gajendra Thakur — 259 
4.2 The Siṃha (Singh) Noble Houses 
A remarkable feature of the Uṭīp is its documentation of the Khaṇḍabalā 
Siṃha clan — a noble Kāyastha family of great standing whose daughters 
married into Brahmin families. Entries repeatedly reference: 
• 
Vābu Ghaṇeśvara Siṃha (vābū ghaneśvara siṃha) of Khaṇḍabalā — a 
frequently cited noble patron 
• 
Vābu Rāmacandra Siṃha, Mahārāja Mādhava Siṃha, Mahārāja Cheha 
Siṃha — successive noble rulers 
• 
Vābu Tañjheśvara Siṃha, Vābu Vasudeva Siṃha, Vābu Prājāpati Siṃha 
— landed nobility 
• 
Rāja Vijaya Govinda Siṃha — explicitly titled as a Raja with political 
functions 
• 
Mahārājā Mahiṣvara Siṃha and Mahārājādhirāja Rāmeśvara Siṃha 
Bahādura — indicating a ruling family of considerable importance 
The documentation of inter-caste alliances through marriage (Brahmin 
grooms, Kāyastha noble families as bride-donors or related families) 
illustrates how the Pañjī captures not just religious but political-social 
alliances. The noble Siṃha families of Khaṇḍabalā functioned as patrons of 
Brahmin learning and their genealogy was tracked precisely because 
matrimonial connections to them conferred prestige. 
4.3 Scholars of National and International Distinction 
Several individuals documented in the Uṭīp achieved extraordinary 
distinction in the modern academic world, demonstrating the continuity of 
the community's scholastic tradition from classical to modern times: 
Mahāmahopādhyāya Dr. Sir Gaṅgānātha Jhā (m.m. ḍā. sara 
gaṃgānātha):Vice Chancellor of Allahabad University; Professor at Oxford 
University, England. One of the greatest Sanskrit scholars of the 20th 
century. His works on Indian philosophy (Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Prābhākara) are 
foundational. The Uṭīp records his gotra, paternal lineage (from Mahiṣī Pālī 
village), and his sons — Dr. Bhavānātha, Dr. Prof. Amarnātha, Dr. 
Śivanātha, Dr. Vibhūtinātha, and Dr. Ādityānātha. 
Dr. Prof. Amarnātha Jhā is recorded as "Proof in Oxford University England 
- V.C. Allahabad University" — the proof here likely meaning "Professor," 
indicating he too rose to the Vice Chancellorship.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 260 
Dr. Vibhūtinātha is recorded as "L. Colonel Deputy Governor of Delhi, 
Town Planner of Delhi" — documenting a lineage member who served in 
senior civil/military administration. 
Prof. Meṣeśvara (M.A., Ph.D., D.Litt.):Professor; the document records his 
village of Māni-Saudāpur and his son Sañjaya. 
Prof. (Ānaṃda) Gaṃgādhara:M.A. in Hindi, Maithili, Economics; 
Department Head of Patna College. Documented in the Digauṃdh lineage. 
Pro. Govinda and Pro. Jagadīśa:Both identified as "Maithilī Sāhityakāra" 
(Maithili literary authors), documenting creative writers within the 
genealogical record. 
This seamless integration of medieval scholarly titles (Mahāmahopādhyāya), 
professional credentials (M.B.B.S., D.G.P., C.S.M.B.B.S., L.L.B.), and 
traditional categories (Vaidyakaraṇī, Jyautiṣī) within a single document 
illustrates how the Pañjī evolved to absorb modernity while maintaining its 
pre-modern classificatory logic. 
4.4 Modern Professionals Documented 
The Uṭīp conscientiously documents modern professional identities alongside 
traditional ones, reflecting the community's transition into modernity: 
• 
D.G.P. Bhīmanātha (Director General of Police — a senior Indian Police 
Service officer) 
• 
Civil Surgeon (medical officer of senior rank in colonial/post-colonial 
administration) 
• 
M.B.B.S. doctors (multiple instances — Snehāṃśu Pratāpa, Dr. Ṛṣiśvara, 
etc.) 
• 
Engineers — "Mukhya Abhiyantā" (Chief Engineer) and "E. Sureśa" 
(Engineer Sureśa) 
• 
Vakīl (lawyer/advocate) — noted as Pañcānanda, Jagadīśa 
• 
Dena Bank Chairman — noted against Śambhūpati's son Lakṣmaṇa 
• 
I.A.S. (Indian Administrative Service) officer — Maṃtheśvara, noted in 
the Gaṃgaurā lineage 
• 
Sāhityakāra (literary authors) — multiple individuals noted as Maithili 
writers 
The inclusion of these details is remarkable: it shows that the Pañjīkāra 
updated records with modern professional achievements, treating an IAS

Gajendra Thakur — 261 
posting or a medical degree as socially significant genealogical data 
comparable to a traditional scholarly title. 
5. GEOGRAPHIC COVERAGE: THE MITHILA KINSHIP 
MAP 
5.1 Village Clusters and Sub-circuits 
The Uṭīp is organized into geographic sub-circuits, each representing a 
cluster of villages whose lineages are recorded together because of their 
historical matrimonial connections. The major circuits documented in this 
volume include:

Joṃkī, and adjacent hamlets

Khāṛarāta

5.2 Migration Patterns 
By tracing which villages consistently appear as bride-sources (saṃ) versus 
groom-sources, the Uṭīp allows reconstruction of marriage migration 
patterns. Key observations: 
Darbhangā (Darahārā) appears as both a donor and recipient village, 
confirming its status as a major urban center where multiple lineages 
converged. Khaṇḍabalā functions primarily as a donor village — its Siṃha 
noble houses gave daughters to Brahmin scholars across many circuits. 
Sārisab, Māṇḍar, Pābaulī, and Saudāpur are high-frequency bilateral nodes 
— indicating dense, long-term marriage exchange between these villages. 
Villages in Nepal's Terai (referenced obliquely through "Nepal" mentions 
and cross-references to Nepalese Maithili texts) are linked into the network, 
confirming the system's trans-national geographic span. 
The migration of individual families is also directly documented: entries 
noting a family "moving to" (ujāna — upstream or distant settlement) a new 
location, or being categorized as Pūrvārīpāra (eastern circuit) versus 
Paścimārīpāra (western circuit), create a spatial map of demographic 
movement within Mithila over centuries. 
6. THE PAÑJĪ AS ANTHROPOLOGICAL INSTITUTION 
6.1 A System of Social Verification and Identity Control 
The Pañjī Prabandha is, in anthropological terms, one of the world's most 
sophisticated systems of identity management. Comparable institutions 
include: 
• 
Chinese clan genealogies (zǔpǔ) — which are, however, primarily 
archival and commemorative 
• 
European noble genealogies maintained by Heralds — primarily 
concerned with property and title 
• 
Jewish oral genealogies (yichus) — more status-conferring than 
regulating 
The Pañjī is unique in combining all three functions — archival, status-
conferring, and actively regulatory — with a professional maintenance class 
(Pañjīkāra), formal cross-referencing, and binding social authority. No

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 263 
marriage among regulated Maithili Brahmin families could be solemnized 
without Pañjī clearance. 
6.2 The Pañjīkāra as Social Specialist 
The Pañjīkāra family (or families) who maintained these records functioned 
as a distinctive social sub-caste. Their knowledge was hereditary, 
professional, and monopolistic. From the anthropological evidence in this 
text: 
• 
They maintained multiple physical volumes (the cross-references span 
several hundred pages in each volume) 
• 
They memorized lineage trees to a depth of 15–20 generations 
• 
They officiated at marriage negotiations as expert witnesses 
• 
They charged fees for their services (this is documented in historical 
accounts of the system) 
• 
Their authority was recognized across the entire Mithila region, 
transcending local political boundaries 
The Uṭīp itself represents the Pañjīkāra's working notes — the supplementary 
register needed when the main Pañjī was too general to capture all branches. 
Its existence signals the system's adaptive capacity: as families proliferated 
and migrated, the Pañjī expanded accordingly. 
6.3 Gender in the Pañjī System 
The Pañjī is, structurally, a patrilineal document: it traces descent through the 
male line and records women primarily as connection points between 
lineages (as daughters of X who married Y). Women appear in the Pañjī only 
through their fathers and husbands. 
However, this is not to say women were genealogically invisible. On the 
contrary: 
• 
A woman's gotra and mūl are crucial data for marriage clearance (she 
must not share her husband's gotra) 
• 
The maternal line (the ddau side) is recorded at every generation — 
meaning every man's mother's father's lineage is documented 
• 
"Dampati lekhaka" (husband-wife writers) traditions documented in 
Videha archives show that women did participate in literary production

Gajendra Thakur — 264 
• 
The "Gośāunī putrī" (daughter of Gośāuṃnī) entries and "sutāśca" (sons 
and daughters) notations acknowledge female progeny even if not 
genealogically traced further 
6.4 Polygamy, Succession, and Social Complexity 
The repeated "apara" (second wife, third wife) entries reveal that polygamy 
was normalized and systematically documented among the elite Brahmin 
families recorded here. Observations: 
• 
Multiple wives were common — entries with three or four "apara" wives 
per man appear 
• 
Each wife's children are carefully separated and their maternal lineages 
independently verified 
• 
Half-siblings from different mothers are genealogically distinguished 
even though they share the same father 
• 
Childlessness (some entries trail off with "saṃ ... suta ... dau ... saṃ ... 
ddau" with blanks) is recorded but not stigmatized 
This systematic polygamy documentation reflects a society where elite male 
status enabled multiple marriages, but also where the resulting genealogical 
complexity required institutional management — which the Pañjī provided. 
7. LINGUISTIC AND TEXTUAL ANALYSIS 
7.1 The Language of the Pañjī 
The text is written in Classical Maithili in its specialized genealogical 
register — a formal, highly compressed dialect distinct from literary or 
spoken Maithili. Key features: 
• 
Archaic Maithili grammatical forms (suto/sutau/suta for 
singular/dual/plural children) 
• 
Sanskrit-derived technical vocabulary (gotra, pravara, mūla, Vaidika, 
Māmāṃśaka) 
• 
Village-name inflections in the ablative (Sārisab saṃ = "from Sārisab") 
• 
Extreme compression — each entry encodes 4–5 generations of 
genealogical data in 2–3 lines 
• 
Numbers in parentheses as cross-references — an indigenous citation 
system

records.text:

• 
Abbreviations: pr./pro. for prāyukta (alias), Ut. for Uṭīp, Sha. for Śāka 
era dating 
The document's dating references alternate between two systems: Śāka era 
(śāo — adding approximately 78 years to get Common Era year) and the 
Uṭīp's own internal numbering, indicating the system was maintained across 
multiple historical periods using period-appropriate dating conventions. 
7.2 The Formula as Cultural Code 
The standardized genealogical formula — "[Person] suto [Child]: [Village] 
saṃ [Name] suta [Name] dau [Village] saṃ [Name] ddau" — is a cultural 
code of extraordinary density. A single line encodes: 
• 
Paternal descent (father's name, grandfather's name) 
• 
Village of domicile (current family location) 
• 
Mother's paternal lineage (maternal grandfather's name and his village) 
• 
Maternal great-grandfather's lineage (ddau side, second degree) 
• 
Cross-reference to main Pañjī volume for verification 
This formula has remained stable for centuries — entries from the early 
Śāka-era sections use the same grammatical structure as entries from 2009, 
demonstrating the conservatism of the institutional genre. 
7.3 Devanagari Script and Maithili Orthography 
The document uses Devanagari script with Maithili-specific orthographic 
conventions. Notable features include the use of the anusvāra (nasalization 
mark) in positions unusual for standard Hindi or Sanskrit, and the 
preservation of certain old Maithili phonological distinctions. The Tirhuta 
script (native to Mithila) is not used in this volume, which is printed in 
Devanagari for wider accessibility — consistent with the Videha movement's 
biliteracy approach. 
8. THE "GENOME MAPPING" METAPHOR: A 
CRITICAL ANALYSIS 
8.1 Why "Genome"? 
The title's invocation of "genome mapping" is not accidental or merely 
ornamental. It represents a deliberate intellectual argument made by the 
editor (Gajendra Thakur) and the Videha Parallel Literature Movement:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 266 
• 
Like a genome, the Pañjī is a complete, continuous, and self-referencing 
record of biological and social inheritance 
• 
Like genomic data, Pañjī information is used to make decisions about 
compatibility and future inheritance 
• 
Like genomic mapping projects (which reconstruct evolutionary history), 
the Pañjī traces historical lineages with precision 
• 
Like genomic databases, the Pañjī is maintained by specialists, cross-
referenced, and updated continuously 
8.2 The Biocultural Argument 
The metaphor makes an implicit biocultural argument: that the Pañjī system 
has, for 1, years, been performing the same function as modern population 
genetics — tracking heritable traits (in this case, social and ritual traits 
defined as heritable: gotra, pravara, mūl) across a large, geographically 
dispersed population. 
From an anthropological standpoint, this is a defensible claim. The Pañjī has, 
in effect, created a centuries-long natural experiment in endogamy and 
kinship regulation, the demographic consequences of which could in 
principle be analyzed using modern tools. The 450 A.D.–2009 A.D. span 
represents approximately 50–60 generations — a genealogical dataset of 
extraordinary depth. 
8.3 The Political Argument 
The metaphor also serves a political function within the Videha movement's 
agenda: it asserts the scientific and scholarly equivalence of indigenous 
knowledge systems with Western academic disciplines. By framing the Pañjī 
as "genome mapping," the text claims for it the prestige of modern science 
while insisting it is genuinely indigenous — a rebuttal to the colonial-era 
dismissal of traditional Indian knowledge as unscientific or merely 
superstitious.

9. THE VIDEHA PARALLEL LITERATURE 
MOVEMENT: CONTEXT 
9.1 Counter-Canonical Scholarship 
This document is published under the aegis of the Videha Parallel Literature 
Movement, founded by Gajendra Thakur, editor of Videha (videha.co.in), the 
first Maithili fortnightly eJournal. The Movement is explicitly positioned as a 
counter-canonical alternative to the Sahitya Akademi institutional authority. 
The decision to publish a work like the Pañjī Uṭīp within this counter-
canonical framework is significant: it asserts that the Pañjī tradition — 
marginalized by mainstream Maithili literary institutions as "mere 
genealogy" — is in fact a sophisticated literary and scholarly genre deserving 
serious academic attention. 
9.2 Digital Preservation as Political Act 
By digitizing, editing, and making accessible the Pañjī Uṭīp through the 
Videha platform, Thakur performs an act of cultural preservation with 
political dimensions: 
• 
Endangered knowledge: The Pañjīkāra tradition is declining as younger 
generations abandon the hereditary profession 
• 
Digital accessibility: Moving from manuscript/printed register to digital 
text vastly increases accessibility 
• 
Linguistic assertion: Publishing in Maithili (rather than Hindi or English) 
asserts the language's sufficiency for serious scholarship 
• 
Community memory: For Maithili Brahmin families scattered across 
North Bihar and the diaspora, digital Pañjī access reconnects them to 
genealogical roots 
9.3 Scholarly Innovation 
The framing of the Pañjī as "Genome Mapping" and the integration of 
modern credentials (IAS, D.G.P., M.B.B.S.) alongside medieval titles 
(Mahāmahopādhyāya, Vaidyakaraṇī) within the same institutional record is 
itself a form of scholarly innovation — a demonstration that indigenous 
knowledge systems have the capacity to absorb and incorporate modernity on 
their own terms, without dissolving into it.

10. HISTORICAL DEPTH: TEMPORAL LAYERS IN THE 
DOCUMENT 
10.1 Śāka Era Dating 
The document uses Śāka era dates (denoted by "Sha." or śāo) for older 
entries. The Śāka era begins in 78 A.D. Therefore: 
• 
Śāka 78 = 156 A.D. 
• 
Śāka 800 = 878 A.D. 
• 
Śāka 1900 = 1978 A.D. 
References to Śāka dates in the 100–300 range (corresponding to 
approximately 178–378 A.D.) would place some lineage origins in the pre-
Gupta to early Gupta period — consistent with the document's claim of 
coverage from 450 A.D. References in the 1900+ range align with the late 
20th century, when the most recent entries were recorded. 
10.2 Evidence of Medieval Lineages 
Several entries document individuals with classical Sanskrit scholarly titles 
(Māmāṃśaka, Vaidyakaraṇī, Jyautiṣī) who would have flourished in the 
medieval period (roughly 1000–1700 A.D.). The Pañjī's capacity to trace 
from these scholars to their living 20th-century descendants is its most 
remarkable historical feature — it provides a continuous thread through the 
medieval and early modern periods for which other historical documentation 
of ordinary (non-royal) families is extremely scarce. 
10.3 Continuity Through Political Disruption 
The period 450–2009 A.D. encompasses the Gupta Empire, the Pāla dynasty, 
Sultanate rule, Mughal suzerainty, British colonial administration, and post-
Independence India — multiple complete transformations of the political 
order. Through all of this, the Pañjī system continued. This continuity itself is 
a major historical fact: it demonstrates that the Maithili Brahmin community 
maintained institutional self-governance through kinship and matrimonial 
regulation that survived political disruption. The Pañjī is thus also a history 
of community resilience and institutional durability.

11. OBSERVATIONS AND CONCLUSIONS 
11.1 What This Document Tells Us About Mithila 
Reading this Uṭīp carefully yields the following observations of 
anthropological, sociological, and historical significance: 
• 
Mithila's Brahmin community was extraordinarily geographically mobile 
within the region — families moved between dozens of villages across 
multiple generations, but maintained genealogical connectivity through 
the Pañjī 
• 
Scholarly achievement (Sanskrit grammar, philosophy, astrology, poetry) 
was not individual but lineage-embedded — the same families produced 
scholars across generations, and this was tracked and celebrated 
• 
The transition from medieval to modern was not disruptive but 
cumulative — modern credentials were added to, not substituted for, 
traditional ones 
• 
Women were genealogically essential though textually marginal — every 
man's maternal lineage was recorded to three degrees, making women 
crucial nodes even within a patrilineal system 
• 
The document reflects a community with high levels of internal social 
differentiation that was carefully managed through an information 
system rather than through political coercion 
11.2 The Document's Significance for Genealogical Scholarship 
This Uṭīp represents one of the most substantial collections of continuous 
genealogical data available for any South Asian community. Its significance 
for genealogical scholarship includes: 
• 
Depth: 1, years of continuous lineage data in a single community 
• 
Breadth: Hundreds of families across dozens of villages documented 
simultaneously 
• 
Verification: Cross-referencing system creates internal consistency 
checks 
• 
Modernity integration: Seamless incorporation of post-1947 credentials 
• 
Bi-directional tracing: Both paternal (suta) and maternal (dau/ddau) lines 
documented

11.3 Limitations and Lacunae 
No scholarly report would be complete without acknowledging limitations: 
• 
Gender bias: Women's own lineages are not traced forward — daughters 
vanish from the record after their marriage entry 
• 
Social exclusivity: The Pañjī documents only Brahmin (and some 
Kāyastha-adjacent) families — the vast majority of Mithila's population 
(Dalit, OBC, Muslim, and other communities) are entirely absent 
• 
Scribal variation: The compression of the format means errors, 
omissions, and ambiguities are difficult to detect without cross-
referencing multiple volumes 
• 
Oral supplement: Much Pañjī knowledge was oral and has not been 
systematically captured in the written record 
• 
Ink of entries: This volume (2009 A.D.) captures a moment — many 
entries trail off with blanks, indicating ongoing recording 
11.4 Final Assessment 
The Jīnom Maipiṃg — Mithilāk Pañjī Prabandha, Volume II, Section 2 
(Uṭīp) is a document of world-class significance for the study of genealogy, 
kinship, social stratification, community identity, and indigenous knowledge 
systems. It represents an unbroken scholarly and social tradition of fifteen 
centuries, maintained by a community that understood — long before 
modern bioinformatics — that tracking hereditary information systematically 
across generations is one of the most powerful tools for social organization. 
Its publication within the Videha Parallel Literature Movement places it 
squarely within a project of cultural recovery and reassertion: the argument 
that the intellectual traditions of Mithila are not mere historical relics but 
living, evolving systems of knowledge production that deserve the same 
scholarly attention as any academic discipline. The "genome mapping" 
metaphor is thus both analytically precise and politically meaningful — a 
claim that indigenous science is science, and that the Pañjī Prabandha is one 
of humanity's great unrecognized intellectual achievements.

Chapter 13 
Genome Mapping Volume I: Analytical Report on 
Mithilak Pañjee Prabandha 
GENOME MAPPING OF MITHILA 
An Analytical Report on Volume II of 
Jeenoam Maeping (450 A.D. – 2009 A.D.) — Mithilak Panjee 
Prabandha 
A Comprehensive Scholarly Examination in Anthropology, Sociology, and 
Genealogy 
Prepared as an Explanatory Academic Report 
Prefatory Note: What This Document Is 
The uploaded document, bearing the title Jeenoam Maeping (450 A.D. – 
2009 A.D.) – Mithilak Panjee Prabandha, is Volume II (Khanda 2) of a 
monumental genealogical compilation concerning the Brahmin (Maithil 
Brahmin) community of the Mithila region in the present-day states of Bihar 
and Jharkhand in India, as well as parts of the Terai region of Nepal. The 
work represents a continuation — likely one of multiple volumes — of what 
the editors and compilers call a 'Genome Mapping' of Mithila across 
approximately fifteen centuries, from the mid-fifth century of the Common 
Era to the first decade of the twenty-first century. 
This report explains, in full scholarly depth, what the document is, what 
tradition it belongs to, what information it encodes, how that information is 
structured, what social and cultural realities it reflects, and why such a 
document is of extraordinary significance in the fields of historical

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 272 
anthropology, Indological studies, social history, caste sociology, and the 
theory of oral and textual genealogical knowledge systems. 
Section I: The Panjee Tradition — Origins and Historical 
Context 
1.1 What Is the Panjee System? 
The Panjee Prabandha (literally 'genealogical administration' or 'genealogical 
management') is a centuries-old institution unique to the Maithil Brahmin 
community of the Mithila cultural zone. The word 'Panjee' derives from the 
Sanskrit/Maithili root meaning 'register' or 'record.' The institution is, in 
essence, a living database of patrilineal descent, managed by a hereditary 
class of professional genealogists called Panji Bads or Panjeekar. 
The origins of the Panjee system are traditionally ascribed to the reign of 
Harsimha Deva (circa 1301–1324 CE), a king of the Karnat dynasty who 
ruled Mithila from Simraon. According to tradition, he commissioned the 
first systematic genealogical registration of Maithil Brahmin families to 
regulate marriage alliances and prevent violations of gotra (patrilineal clan) 
and pravara (lineage categories within a gotra) exogamy rules. The system 
has been maintained — with updates, additions, and revisions — ever since, 
making it one of the oldest continuously operative genealogical registries in 
the world. 
The document's subtitle calls the work a 'Genome Mapping,' deploying a 
contemporary scientific metaphor deliberately: just as modern genomics 
maps the genetic lineages of populations, the Panjee system maps social and 
genealogical lineages in explicit, codified, and verifiable form. 
1.2 The Maithil Brahmin Community: Sociological Overview 
The Maithil Brahmins are a sub-group of the broader Brahmin varna (the 
priestly-scholarly class in the Hindu caste hierarchy). They are associated 
specifically with the Mithila region, a cultural-geographical area centred on 
the ancient city of Videha/Mithila (modern Sitamarhi, Madhubani, 
Darbhanga, and Muzaffarpur districts of Bihar). Maithili is their mother 
tongue, one of the recognized literary languages of India, belonging to the 
Magadhan Prakrit family.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 273 
Within the Maithil Brahmin community, the Panjee system applies 
specifically to those who have registered their families within it — known as 
Shrotriyas (those who follow Vedic learning and tradition). The document 
deals with lineages traced across numerous villages, collectively constituting 
what scholars call the panjikar's 'territory' (khanda or division). Volume II 
represents a particular territorial and genealogical bloc. 
The community is itself sub-divided by numerous criteria that the Panjee 
system meticulously tracks: gotra (exogamous patrilineal clan, tracing back 
to a Vedic sage), mool (the original ancestral village or founding site), mula-
purusha (founding progenitor), and shreni (quality-rank within the 
community, e.g., Shrotriya, Yogya, or Jayyach). 
1.3 The Professional Genealogist (Panjeekar) 
The Panjeekar (keeper of the Panjee) is a hereditary professional genealogist 
whose family has maintained the records of specific segments of the Maithil 
Brahmin population across generations. A Panjeekar does not merely copy 
records; he actively updates them by visiting client families, recording births, 
deaths, marriages, and migrations, and encoding this information in the 
compact formula-notation that the Panjee employs. His authority is 
recognized by the community: no marriage can be contracted between two 
Maithil Brahmin families without consulting the relevant Panjeekar to verify 
that no prohibited relationship exists. 
The Panjee as a system thus functions as both a historical archive and a 
living regulatory institution — a 'genealogical constitution' of the 
community, to use a modern analogy. 
Section II: Structure and Format of the Document 
2.1 The Title and Volume Context 
The document is paginated as pages 2. through 2., indicating it is an internal 
section of the second khanda (volume/section) of a larger work. The running 
header reads: 'Jeenoam Maeping (450 A.D. se 2009 A.D.) – Mithilak Panjee 
Prabandha' — 'Genome Mapping (from 450 CE to 2009 CE) — Genealogical 
Administration of Mithila.' 
The subheading on each page reads 'Khanda 2 — Upatip' (Volume 2 — Sub-
record/Annotation). The term Upatip (from Sanskrit upa = 'secondary' or

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 274 
'supplementary' + tip = 'note') signals that these are detailed sub-records or 
glosses, as opposed to the main tabular Panjee records. This is a recognized 
division within Panjee scholarship: the main Panjee contains compressed 
entries, and the Upatip contains expanded genealogical notes with fuller 
family histories. 
2.2 Geographical Organization 
The entries in Volume II are organized geographically — by village (gram) 
of origin or residence. Each block of entries is introduced by a heading 
announcing the gram and the administrative direction (purvari par = eastern 
bank; pashchari par = western bank) or the paar (territorial division). This 
reflects the fact that the Panjee system's territorial organization follows river 
systems and directional quadrants: villages on the eastern and western banks 
of rivers like the Kamla, Bagmati, Kosi, and their tributaries are treated as 
distinct units. 
In the segment of the document we have, the following villages and localities 
appear as major nodes: 
Fawaripar Satoli — Gangoli Najira (entries 155–158) 
Purvari Par Rajora — Rampur (entries 159–161) 
Sihauli — Lohana, Charou (entries 162–175) 
Sulhani — Manglabani, Naruaar (entries 183–194) 
Pude — Mahaail, Koilarbaha (entries 197–207) 
Bhour — Rajgram, Hemnagarr (entries 215–224) 
Loam — Sugaun, Dugaganj (entries 208–213) 
Kandhouli — Katihaar (entries 213–214) 
Each village name is followed by genealogical entries for the families traced 
to that village. The cross-referencing between villages is constant: families 
moved, married across localities, and the Panjee tracks both the home village 
(mool grama) and the current village of residence. 
2.3 The Grammatical-Notational System 
The Panjee uses a highly compressed, formulaic notation in Maithili-Sanskrit 
mixed script (Devanagari). Understanding this notation is the key to reading 
the document. The following is a systematic decoding:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 275 
Core Genealogical Terms 
Suta / Sutou / Suta:Son / Two sons / Sons (plural). This is the primary 
descent marker. 'X suta Y' means 'X's son is Y.' 
Dau / Dou:The father-in-law's side (the bridegroom's father / the person 
through whom the wife is given). Encodes the marriage alliance: 'X suta Y, 
Village A se Z suta W dau' = 'X's son Y was given in marriage [to a bride] 
from Village A, [specifically through] the lineage of Z's son W [as the 
officiating party].' 
Ddau (written as d^dou):The maternal grandfather's line (nana paksha). 
Double-dau encodes the maternal genealogy of the bride.' 
Pr. / Pra.:Abbreviation for 'prakhyaat' = 'known as,' 'alias,' or 'popular name.' 
Maithil Brahmins commonly had a formal name and a familiar nickname. 
E.g., 'Feku pr. Dinabandhu' = 'Feku, known formally as Dinabandhu.' 
Apara:'Another [entry for the same person],' indicating an alternative 
alliance or additional genealogical information.' 
M.M.U. / Maho.:'Mahaamahopaadhyaaya' — a very high scholarly-
honorific title indicating a person of great learning; equivalent to a 
Distinguished Professor or highest academic honor. Entries bearing M.M.U. 
signal an individual of outstanding scholarly distinction.' 
Vaidyak / Vaiya.:Abbreviation for 'Vaiyaakarana' — a specialist in Sanskrit 
grammar (Vyaakarana). Marks a person as a professional grammarian.' 
Nauyi / Naiyayik:A specialist in Nyaya philosophy (logic and 
epistemology). Marks a professional logician.' 
Jyotirvidh / Jyo.:An astrologer-astronomer.' 
Sahityakaar:A literary author or poet.' 
Kavi:Poet.' 
S. / Sa.:'Swaami' (gentleman, master) or a rank indicator.' 
Thakur / Th.:A title of social honor, particularly for Kayastha and upper-
caste families who are not Brahmins but appear in the Panjee as marriage 
alliance partners; also used for Brahmins of the kshatriya-aligned Bhumihar 
class.' 
Babu / Vabu:'Gentleman/Master,' a honorific for zamindars (landed gentry), 
rajas, and men of rank. Entries reading 'Vabu X Singh' or 'Babu X Singh' 
indicate landed aristocracy.'

Gajendra Thakur — 276 
Maharaj:'Great King,' used for actual royalty or major zamindars of quasi-
regal status.' 
Raja:'King,' applied to regional rulers and large zamindars.' 
Ray / Rai:A title of the Kayastha (administrative/clerical caste) upper class; 
also used for distinguished Brahmin scholars granted this title by rulers.' 
Da. / Daa.:'Doctor' (Da. = Dr.), appearing in later entries indicating modern 
medical or academic degrees (e.g., 'Da. Shyamsundar MBBS CS').' 
M.A. / Ph.D.:Modern academic degrees, appearing in entries from the 
twentieth century.' 
I.A.S. / I.P.S.:Indian Administrative Service / Indian Police Service — elite 
civil service cadres, their mention signals exceptional social achievement.' 
Lt. Colonel:Military rank, appearing in the twentieth-century entries.' 
Reference Numbers 
Each genealogical entry contains references in the format '(U.T. xxx/yy)' or 
'(Ra. xxx/yy)'. These are cross-references to specific entries in the main 
Panjee register: 
U.T. = Upatip (the supplementary annotation volume itself, cross-referencing 
back to other entries). 
Ra. = The main register number and sub-entry number. 
The number before the slash is the main register folio or entry number; the 
number after is a sub-entry or variant. 
These cross-references are the connective tissue of the system — they link 
scattered families across the entire corpus of Panjee volumes. 
2.4 Directional Indicators and Territorial Divisions 
Entries frequently mention: 
Purvari par = eastern bank (of a river, used as a territorial marker). 
Pashchari par / Fawaripar = western bank. 
Ujan = upstream. 
*Grambhog = the'gram-bhojan,'i.e., a village's inauguration or establishment 
marker.* 
These terms establish spatial coordinates within the Panjee's organizational 
grid. Mithila's genealogical geography is structured along river systems

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 277 
(Kamla, Bagmati, Kosi, Gandak), and the directional indicators tell the 
reader on which bank and in what direction from a reference point the village 
lies. 
Section III: Content Analysis — What the Entries Tell Us 
3.1 Patrilineal Descent and the Male Line 
The foundational logic of the Panjee is agnatic (patrilineal) — descent flows 
exclusively through males. Every entry records a father-to-son chain. The 
depth of genealogical memory encoded is remarkable: in many entries, six to 
nine generations of continuous male descent are recorded within a single 
compressed line. 
A typical entry reads (transliterated and translated): 'Rananath suta 
Shivankara pra. Rudranath Harshankara pra. Adyanath Dayankara 
Samankara Shambhushankara Vijayashankaraa: Khandbala se Khagpati 
Singh suta Vabu Harshpati Singh dau (Ra. 294/102) Hariyambha se 
Panchamani ddau.' 
Decoded: The sons of Rananath are Shivankara (alias Rudranath), 
Harshankara (alias Adyanath), Dayankara, Samankara, Shambhushankara, 
and Vijayashankara. [Concerning their marriage alliance:] From the village 
Khandbala, [they married into the family of] Khagpati Singh's son Vabu 
Harshpati Singh [as the dau-party, i.e., the bride-giving side]. Cross-
reference: Register 294, sub-entry 102. On the Hariyambha side, Panchamani 
is the maternal grandfather's representative (ddau-party). 
The information density is extraordinary. In one formulaic sentence, we 
learn: six sons of one man; the village of the groom's family; the names of 
two generations on the bride-giving side; and two cross-reference numbers 
linking to full entries elsewhere. 
3.2 Marriage Alliance Networks (Kula and Rista) 
The Panjee's deepest sociological function is to map marriage alliances. 
Every genealogical entry records, for each male member, the marriage 
alliance through which his wife came — specifically identifying the bride's 
father's family (dau side) and the maternal grandfather's family (ddau side). 
This dual recording reflects the Maithil Brahmin custom of tracking three 
generations of genealogical distance to prevent close-kin marriage.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 278 
The villages that appear as dau and ddau partners in the document represent 
the actual geographical range of marriage networks. From the entries, we can 
identify that families from the following villages routinely intermarried: 
Saurisab (Sarisab): appears dozens of times as a dau or ddau source village. 
Paboli (Paboli/Paholi): similarly frequent. 
Karimahal (Karimaha): very frequent — one of the major alliance villages. 
Niyaun (Niuan): appears constantly as a marriage source. 
Sodarpur (Sodaipur): extremely frequent throughout. 
Khandbala (Khandbala): frequent, and associated with several Babu/Raja 
families. 
Hariyambha: appears throughout as a ddau source. 
Dariharaa (Darbhanga area): consistent presence. 
Pali: very frequent across all sections. 
Maandaar (Mandar): frequent. 
Bujuwal, Kujowuli, Paholi, Saksarhi, Ekahar, Pandua: all repeatedly named. 
This inter-village marriage network, when mapped graphically, would reveal 
the precise geographic extent of the Maithil Brahmin matrimonial universe 
— and by extension, their social solidarity network — across at least the 
districts of Madhubani, Darbhanga, Sitamarhi, Muzaffarpur, Samastipur, and 
parts of Begusarai in Bihar. 
3.3 Titles, Professions, and Social Ranks 
One of the most ethnographically rich aspects of the document is its 
recording of titles and professions alongside genealogical data. These 
annotations reveal the occupational and status differentiation within the 
Maithil Brahmin community across centuries. 
Scholarly Titles 
The title M.M.U. (Mahaamaahopaadhyaaya) appears multiple times, 
indicating individuals who attained the highest recognition in Sanskrit 
learning. One entry mentions 'M.M.U. Sadananda' and 'M.M.U. Sadan suta 
Maho. Shashthiinath Mishra,' signalling a family of multi-generational 
scholars. The Vaiyaakarana (grammarian) title appears for specialists in 
Sanskrit grammar — a highly prestigious specialization in Mithila, which 
was for centuries a major center of Sanskrit grammatical scholarship (the

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 279 
Maithil school of grammar, represented by figures like Chandradhar Mishra, 
was nationally renowned). 
The Naaiyayik (Nyaya logician) designation, appearing for example in 
'Naiyayik Maho. Durgadhar suta Nagesh Vidyadharo,' identifies specialists in 
the Nyaya-Vaisheshika philosophical-logical tradition, for which Mithila was 
the most important center in India from the twelfth to eighteenth centuries — 
home to the philosopher Gangesa Upadhyaya (c. 1325 CE), founder of the 
Navya-Nyaya (New Logic) school. 
Royal and Aristocratic Titles 
The document records multiple families bearing the title Raja and Maharaja: 
'Maharaj Maarav Singh suta Maharaj Chhatrasingh,' 'Raja Devanarayana suta 
Raja Rupanarayana,' 'Raja Lilananada Singh.' These are not emperors but 
regional rulers and zamindars (landed aristocrats) — the holders of large 
estates (talukas, villages) under the Mughal, British, and post-independence 
revenue systems. 
The Babu/Vabu title (used extensively for the Khandbala estate's Singh 
family: 'Vabu Samapati Singh,' 'Vabu Khagapati Singh,' 'Vabu Harshpati 
Singh,' 'Vabu Ganesha Singh,' etc.) marks a prominent zamindari lineage 
whose members held social power in the Mithila region over several 
centuries. 
Similarly, 
the 
Ray 
families 
(like 
'Ray 
Chilka 
pra. 
Chandranaarayan') are from the Kayastha administrative class, indicating that 
the Panjee tracked inter-caste alliances as well. 
Modern Professional Titles 
In entries from the twentieth century, the Panjee incorporates modern 
credentials alongside traditional ones, demonstrating the institution's adaptive 
capacity: 'Da. Shyamsundar (M.B.B.S., C.S.),' 'M.A., Ph.D.,' 'I.A.S.' (Indian 
Administrative Service), 'I.P.S.' (Indian Police Service), 'Lt. Colonel 
Gurakar,' 'Prof.,' 'Dr. Pro. Principal (B.N. College, Patna).' One entry names 
'Soc. Prof. Ugranath' (a professor of sociology). This intergenerational 
continuity of recording — from Sanskrit pandits to MBBS doctors to IAS 
officers — across fifteen centuries within a single notational framework is 
without parallel in any known genealogical system.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 280 
3.4 Female Presence in a Patrilineal Document 
While the Panjee is fundamentally patrilineal, women are not entirely absent 
— they appear structurally, as the pivot of the alliance system. Every 
dau/ddau recording implicitly encodes a woman: she is the person through 
whom the two families are connected. The bride is never named — she is 
genealogically invisible in the patrilineal chain — but the families from 
which she comes are always recorded. This structural invisibility of women, 
combined with the structural centrality of their movement between families, 
is one of the most sociologically significant features of the system. 
In some entries, we glimpse widows, second wives (through 'apara' second-
alliance entries), and in very rare cases, women's names appear in the context 
of female lineages or matrilineal cross-references in the ddau entries. The 
Maithil system, unlike some North Indian hypergamous systems, maintains 
strict records of both bride-giving and bride-taking across multiple 
generations, which paradoxically creates a more complete (if implicit) 
archive of women's genealogical role than systems that record only the male 
line. 
3.5 Migration, Settlement, and Demographic Patterns 
The Panjee entries track geographical mobility. Every entry records both the 
ancestral village (from which a family originates) and the current village 
(where the alliance is contracted). Phrases like 'Gram Aarambha' (beginning 
of the village's record) mark new village entries, indicating when a family 
relocated and established itself in a new place. 
From the document, we can identify several major patterns: 
Movement between the Madhubani, Darbhanga, Sitamarhi, and Muzaffarpur 
districts of Bihar is constant. Families from Sihaoli marry into Lohana; 
families from Rajgram connect with Charou; families from Sulhani spread 
into Manglabani and Naruaar. 
There is a clear directional drift: many families move from eastern areas 
(near the Nepal border) southward and westward toward larger towns and 
administrative centers — Darbhanga, Muzaffarpur, Patna. 
Some entries show families settling in Jhanjharpur, a significant market town 
in Madhubani district, and Champanagar, reflecting urbanization trends. 
Entries for Lalgang (Lalganj), a town near Madhubani, appear in the context 
of twentieth-century families, reflecting post-independence urban migration.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 281 
Nepal-proximate villages (Sugaun, Alai areas) appear frequently, reflecting 
the historical Mithila cultural zone that straddles the modern India-Nepal 
border. 
Section IV: The Social Ecology of the Khandbala Estate 
4.1 The Khandbala Singh Family as a Social Pivot 
Throughout the document, one family stands out as exceptionally prominent: 
the Singh family of Khandbala (Khandabala). In Maithil genealogical terms, 
the Singhs of Khandbala are Bhumihars or high-caste Kayasthas who had 
achieved zamindar (landowning) status. They appear not as Brahmins in the 
Panjee proper but as the marriage-giving family (dau side) for numerous 
Brahmin households — meaning Brahmin families were taking brides from 
or giving brides to the Khandbala estate. 
The names recorded for this family over several generations include: 
Vabu Samapati Singh, Vabu Khagapati Singh, Vabu Harshpati Singh, Vabu 
Prajapati Singh, Vabu Punyapati Singh, Vabu Sureshwara Singh, Vabu 
Harsharani Singh, Vabu Adyadatta Singh, Vabu Giteshwara Singh, Vabu 
Durgapati Singh, Vabu Lalit Singh, Vabu Vijayapati Singh, Vabu Ganesha 
Singh. 
Maharaj Maarav Singh, Maharaj Chhatrasingh, Maharaaja Rudra Singh, 
Maharaj Maheshwar Singh, Maharaj Kameshwar Singh. 
The sheer frequency of the Khandbala family as a marriage-alliance partner 
— appearing in dozens of entries across the document — indicates that this 
zamindari family was one of the central social nodes of the Mithila 
matrimonial universe during the seventeenth through early twentieth 
centuries. Their repeated appearance reflects the social reality of Brahmin-
Bhumihar or Brahmin-Kayastha alliances, which were common in Mithila 
despite theoretical caste endogamy, and which the Panjee faithfully records 
without disapproval. 
4.2 Other Prominent Lineages 
The document records several other families whose genealogical depth and 
social importance are evident from the frequency and elaboration of their 
entries:

The Sadananda lineage from Sulhani Niaun: A family of long-standing 
Sanskrit scholars (M.M.U. Sadananda, M.M.U. Sadde = Sadananda), 
connected to several distinguished branches. 
The Ramanarayana/Devanarayana Royal Ray family of Loam-Sugaun: A 
Kayastha Raja family whose sons bore titles like Raja Devanarayana, Raja 
Rupanarayana, Raja Sriinarayan. Their descendants include Ray Ghagopala

Narayana. 
The Darbhanga Maharaja family: Tangentially referenced through the 
mention of Maharaj Rudra Singh and Maharaj Maheshwar Singh of

*The

Tantranath'— a professor and literary figure — indicating a scholarly family 
that transitioned from Sanskrit to modern academia.* 
Section V: Temporal Depth — Fifteen Centuries of Record 
5.1 The Chronological Range and What It Implies 
The document's subtitle explicitly states coverage from 450 CE to 2009 CE 
— roughly 1, years. This is not rhetorical: the Panjee tradition claims (and 
the oldest layers of the records substantiate) genealogical memory reaching 
back to the period of early medieval Mithila, when the Kayastha-Brahmin 
cultural synthesis that defines Maithil civilization was being consolidated. 
From an anthropological standpoint, this temporal depth is extraordinary. 
Most genealogical systems in the world lose reliable track of descent after 
four to seven generations (roughly 100–175 years). Systems that claim depth 
beyond this typically become mythological — listing legendary ancestors 
rather than historically traceable individuals. The Panjee system's claim to 
fifteen centuries of reliable genealogy is debated by historians, but what is 
not debatable is that the recorded entries from the seventeenth century 
onward are historically verifiable against land records, temple inscriptions, 
Sanskrit manuscripts, and colonial-era survey records. 
In the document before us, entries from what appears to be the seventeenth 
through early twentieth centuries are the most densely populated with 
verifiable data. The earliest layers (pre-1600) are sparser, suggesting that

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:

Gajendra Thakur — 283 
these older entries were reconstructed from fragmentary sources during the 
major Panjee revivals (in the sixteenth and again in the eighteenth-nineteenth 
centuries, during the Darbhanga Maharaja's active patronage of Panjee 
compilation). 
5.2 Dating of Internal Events 
An extraordinary detail appears in one entry: 'Iti Paan Vidyananda 
Suputrasya Abhinava Muraari Pathhadhi Satoli Darihara Upatip Aashaadha 
Sapami Budha — 09..2008 Wednesday.' This is a colophon note — a scribal 
annotation — recording the date and day of completion of a specific Upatip 
section: 9 July 2008, a Wednesday, in the Hindu month of Aashaadha. This 
type of embedded scribal dating is invaluable for scholars: it proves the 
document's compilation is modern (the 2009 endpoint in the title is thus 
corroborated) and shows that active fieldwork for these entries was 
conducted by the Panjeekar as recently as 2008. 
Section VI: The Loam-Sugaun Section — A Case Study in 
Depth 
6.1 The Loa-Sugaun Entries (Sections 208–212) 
The document contains an especially detailed section covering the village of 
Loam (Loa), within the Sugaun area, which records the extensive genealogy 
of the Ray (Rai) family that held the status of local rulers. Entry 208 begins: 
'Atha Loam Sugaun Gram Likhyate — (125/04) Ray Chilka Prasiddha 
Chandranaarayan sutaa Raja Devanarayana Raja Shri Narayana Ray 
Shyamanarayana Ray Lalit Narayana Ray Kirti Narayana:...' 
This 
records 
the 
sons 
of 
the 
distinguished 
Ray 
Chilka 
(alias 
Chandranaarayan): Raja Devanarayana, Raja Shriinarayana (who became a 
Raja), Ray Shyamanarayana, Ray Lalitnaarayan, and Ray Kirtinaaarayan. 
The entry then follows Raja Devanarayana's line for several more 
generations, ultimately recording his descendants in the twentieth century 
bearing names like Ray Vijayanarayana, Ray Chetanarayana, Ray 
Homendranaarayana, Ray Sanjiv, Ray Sameer, down to one who holds the 
title 'I.A.S.' (Naresh Chandra, I.A.S.) — a member of the Indian 
Administrative Service, the elite civil service of independent India.

This single lineage thus compresses in its record the social transformation of 
Mithila from medieval regional kingship (Raja, 17th-18th century) through 
colonial-era zamindari (Ray, 19th century) to post-independence meritocratic 
civil service (I.A.S., 20th century) — three distinct regimes of social power, 
all within one family's recorded genealogy. 
6.2 The Bhour-Rajgram Kshatriya Thakur Families (Sections 
215–224) 
The Bhour-Rajgram section is the most extensive in the document and 
records the genealogy of what appears to be a Kayastha-Kshatriya family of 
considerable local power: the Thakur (Th.) families of the Rajgram area. 
Unlike the Brahmin lineages elsewhere in the document, these families bear 
the title Thakur and their male members use Singh as a suffix — both 
markers of Kshatriya or Kayastha status. 
The foundational figure recorded is 'Bhaiyan pra. Gouriinaath' (Bhaiyan, 
alias Gouriinaath), whose sons are 'Ajit Singh, Udghot Singh, Ujha Singh, 
Ravuni pra. Vujuwal.' The subsequent generations expand dramatically: 
within three generations, the family had multiple branches extending through 
Mohan Singh, Jagat Singh, Damoder Singh, Mahaaraaj Krsna Singh, 
Mahaaraaj Chhatrasingh, and Mahaaraaj Rudra Singh — suggesting a family 
that achieved quasi-regal status by the eighteenth century. 
Particularly notable is the extended treatment of the 'Dharma-Shura 
Mahaamaahopaadhyaaya Krsna Singh' and his descendants — the title 
M.M.U. applied to a Kshatriya (non-Brahmin) individual is exceptional and 
signals a family that had achieved Sanskrit scholastic distinction alongside 
their martial/administrative identity. His sons include Visheshwara Singh, 
Theghnaath Singh, and Kaarinath Singh — the last of whom is followed by 
grandsons bearing modern legislative titles: 'Member of Legislative 
Assembly (Bihar) Kamalnath Singh Thakur' — a democratically elected 
lawmaker in independent India.
