Section VII: The Navya-Nyaya Scholarly Tradition in the 
Entries 
7.1 Mithila as the Center of Indian Logic 
Among the most intellectually significant entries in the document are those 
that record individuals designated as Naiyayika (specialists in Nyaya 
philosophy). The Nyaya school, founded by the sage Gautama, is one of the 
six orthodox systems (astika darshanas) of Hindu philosophy. Its focus is on 
the theory of knowledge (epistemology) and logic. Mithila was, from 
approximately the twelfth century through the eighteenth century, the 
preeminent center of Navya-Nyaya (New Logic) — the reformed, technically 
sophisticated Nyaya philosophy associated above all with Gangesa 
Upadhyaya (c. 1325 CE) of Mithila. 
Entry 165 records: 'Naiyayika Maho. Durgadhara sutou Nagesh Vidyadharo: 
Pali se Nairik Sharanaath suta M.M.U. Da. Sh. Ganganaath dau (U.T. 37/10) 
Sakaradhi se Vaiya. Harshanaath ddau.' This records a distinguished 
scholarly family: 'Naiyayik Maaho. Durgadhara's sons Nagesh and 
Vidyadhara [married into the family of] Nairik Sharanaath's son, the M.M.U. 
Dr. Sir Ganganaath [as the dau party].' 
The mention of 'M.M.U. Da. Sh. Ganganaath' is of the highest historical 
significance: this is almost certainly a reference to Mahamaahopaadhyaaya 
Dr. Sir Ganganatha Jha (1871–1941), one of the greatest Sanskrit scholars of 
the twentieth century, who served as Vice-Chancellor of Allahabad 
University and was knighted ('Sir') by the British Crown for his monumental 
translations of Sanskrit philosophical texts. His appearance in the Panjee as a 
dau-side marriage partner confirms his Maithil Brahmin identity and locates 
him within this very genealogical network. 
7.2 Philosophical-Academic Descent Chains 
The document records entire families of scholars across generations. For 
instance, in Section 204, the Tanaranath family of Pude is recorded as: 
'Tanranath suta: Acharya Ramanath M.A.Ph.D., Sahityakaar Pro. Tantranath, 
Sachinaath, Bodhanath pra. Surial, Ravinaath pra. Sunil.' Acharya 
Ramanath's own sons are listed with their degrees: 'Mrinanath M.A.P.H.D., 
Mahinath M.A.P.H., Naranath M.A.P.M., Duganath M.A.P.H.D., Vidyanath.'

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 286 
This family's trajectory — from the traditional scholarly title Acharya 
(Brahmin teacher) through to multiple PhD holders — exemplifies the 
continuity between the traditional Sanskrit pandita and the modern academic 
professor that characterizes the Maithil intellectual class's engagement with 
modernity. That the Panjee continues to record their genealogy in the same 
format used for medieval pandit families is a testament to the system's 
durability. 
Section VIII: Anthropological and Sociological Significance 
8.1 The Panjee as a Social Technology 
From a sociological perspective, the Panjee system represents one of 
humanity's most elaborate 'social technologies' — a deliberately designed 
institutional mechanism for managing social reproduction. Its primary social 
functions include: 
Exogamy regulation: preventing marriage between relatives within 
prohibited degrees, defined by gotra, pravara, mool, and genealogical 
proximity. 
Status validation: certifying the caste authenticity and social standing of 
families seeking prestigious marriage alliances. 
Alliance memory: preserving the network of existing alliances across 
generations, which has implications for inheritance, ritual obligations, and 
social solidarity. 
Social mobility tracking: recording the rise of families through scholarly 
achievement, political power, or economic success — and, implicitly, the 
decline of families that disappear from the records. 
8.2 The Panjee as an Archive of Social History 
For historians, the Panjee volumes constitute a primary source of 
extraordinary value. The entries in this volume alone document: 
The zamindari system of colonial Bihar, through the frequent mention of 
Babu, Raja, and Maharaja titleholders who were the intermediary landowners 
under British revenue law. 
The emergence of a modern educated class among Maithil Brahmins — the 
doctors, professors, IAS officers, and lawyers — from the late nineteenth 
century onward.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 287 
The persistence of inter-village marriage networks across colonial 
boundaries, suggesting that social life operated at a scale and with a logic 
that the colonial administrative grid only imperfectly captured. 
*The geographical spread of families — from Nepal's Terai through northern 
Bihar — which illuminates the transnational character of Maithil culture long 
before'transnationalism'became an analytical category.* 
8.3 The Panjee and Women's History 
The Panjee presents a profound paradox for feminist historical anthropology: 
it is a document that simultaneously renders women invisible (by name) and 
indispensable (by function). Every family entry is structured around a 
woman's movement — from her natal family to her marital family — as the 
mechanism that creates genealogical connections. The dau and ddau 
formulas encode, in compressed form, the entire system of women's 
exchange that anthropologists call the 'alliance theory of kinship' (associated 
with Claude Lévi-Strauss and Marcel Mauss). 
A rigorous feminist reading of the Panjee would trace the invisible women 
behind every alliance entry. For example, the entry 'X suta Y, Karimahal se Z 
suta W dau, Niyaun se M ddau' records that Y married a woman who came 
from Karimahal (Z's son W is her father or guardian) and whose maternal 
family is from Niyaun (M is the maternal grandfather). Three family 
networks are implicitly encoded in a six-word formula — and at the center of 
all three is an unnamed woman. 
8.4 The Panjee and the Theory of Social Memory 
The Panjee represents what sociologists call 'institutionalized social memory' 
— memory that is externalized from individual minds into a text-based 
system maintained by professionals. Maurice Halbwachs's concept of 
'collective memory' and Jan Assmann's 'cultural memory' both find in the 
Panjee an unusually pure and explicit instantiation. 
Unlike oral genealogies (which are subject to telescoping, inversion, and 
selective forgetting), the Panjee preserves genealogical information in a form 
that resists distortion: its cross-referencing system means that any single 
entry is embedded in a web of mutual citations, making fraudulent alteration 
detectable. The cross-references to other volumes, the registration numbers, 
the colophon dates — all these serve as internal consistency checks that give

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 288 
the system a degree of archival integrity unusual for pre-modern record-
keeping. 
Section IX: Textual and Linguistic Analysis 
9.1 The Language of the Panjee 
The language of the document is a specialized register of Maithili heavily 
inflected with Sanskrit grammatical forms and technical genealogical 
vocabulary. It is neither classical Sanskrit nor colloquial Maithili but a 
professional technical idiom developed specifically for the Panjee tradition. 
Key linguistic features include: 
*Sanskrit case endings applied to Maithili nouns:'Sutah'(nominative 
singular),'Sutou'(nominative dual),'Sutaa:'(nominative plural) — the three 
forms used to indicate one son, two sons, or multiple sons.* 
Sanskrit compounds for titles: Mahaamaahopaadhyaaya (Great Most 
Respected Teacher), Vaiyaakarana (grammarian), Jyotirvidh (astrologer), 
Sahityakaara (literary author). 
*Maithili syntax in the ordering of information: the Subject-Object-Verb 
order and the use of'se'(from) as the ablative marker ('Karimahal se'='from 
Karimahal').* 
Devanagari script with Maithili-specific orthographic conventions: the half-
character forms, the nasal markers, and the vowel matras follow Maithili 
rather than Hindi conventions. 
9.2 The Script and Encoding 
The document uses Devanagari script, the standard writing system for 
Maithili (alongside the indigenous Tirhuta/Mithilakshar script, which the 
Panjee tradition historically used but which has now largely been superseded 
by Devanagari in printed editions). The use of Devanagari for this 
compilation reflects the twentieth-century standardization of Maithili 
typography. The original Panjee manuscripts, preserved by individual 
Panjeekar families, were traditionally written in Tirhuta/Mithilakshar on 
palm leaves or later on paper.

Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 289 
Section X: The Panjee in the Modern World — Continuity 
and Contestation 
10.1 The Panjee's Adaptation to Modernity 
The document before us was compiled or finalized in 2008–2009, as 
evidenced by the internal scribal date. This means that as recently as one 
generation 
ago, 
professional 
Panjiikars 
were 
actively 
conducting 
genealogical surveys of Maithil Brahmin families across Bihar and Nepal, 
recording marriages, births, and professional credentials in the same 
notational system used by their predecessors centuries earlier. 
The inclusion of modern credentials (Ph.D., M.B.B.S., I.A.S., Lt. Colonel) 
alongside traditional titles (M.M.U., Vaiyaakarana, Naiyayik) within the 
same entries demonstrates the Panjee's remarkable adaptive flexibility. 
Rather than becoming a museum artifact, it has continued to evolve as a 
living register — incorporating the new status markers of the modern world 
without abandoning the genealogical logic that gives it its regulatory and 
historical function. 
10.2 Critiques and Contestations 
The Panjee system has not been without controversy. From a sociological 
standpoint, it has been criticized as: 
A mechanism for enforcing caste hierarchy: by making marriage validation 
dependent on Panjee certification, the system maintains Brahmin endogamy 
and excludes inter-caste alliances from legitimate genealogical memory. 
A patriarchal instrument: by recording only male lines and treating women as 
unmarked genealogical conduits, it participates in the systematic erasure of 
women from historical record. 
A class-biased archive: by recording primarily families of sufficient social 
standing to warrant Panjee registration, it systematically underrepresents 
poorer or lower-status Maithil Brahmin families. 
A site of potential manipulation: several studies have documented cases 
where families paid for fraudulent Panjee entries to claim higher social status 
(higher shreni = higher ritual rank within the Brahmin hierarchy). 
These critiques notwithstanding, the Panjee remains one of the most 
significant genealogical archives in the world — invaluable for historical, 
anthropological, demographic, and linguistic research.
